source_metadata.title_raw: Oliver Smith - A-18-200- October 8, 2018 -  Valley Center

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Oliver Smith 
28440 Sunset Road 
Valley Center, CA 92082 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-200 
Dear Mr. Smith: 
October 8, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering 
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are 
complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should 
change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May you participate in the Valley Center Community Planning Group's decision to include 
the installation of a planted median project on the Capital Improvement Projects list for 
consideration by the San Diego County Board of Directors? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Given that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision to place the planted median 
project on the Capital Improvement Projects list will have a material financial effect on your 
interest in the business owned by you and your wife, you are disqualified from taking part in the 
decision. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY THE REQUESTOR 
The Valley Center Community Planning Group ("VCCPG") is chartered by the San Diego 
County Board of Directors to provide recommendations on planning issues for your community. 
You are currently the chair ofVCCPG. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-200 
Page No. 2 
VCCPG is comprised of 15 publicly elected members, has no formal authority to bind San 
Diego County, and its members do not receive compensation. VCCPG members adhere to the 
Brown Act, file Form 700 disclosures, perform county training once a year, and take ethics training 
courses every two years. VCCPG holds regularly scheduled and noticed public meetings on the 
second Monday of each month. 
Each year, VCCPG develops a Capital Improvement Projects ("CIP") list on behalf of the 
community, which is submitted for the County's consideration. VCCPG subcommittees are 
currently preparing an update to the CIP list. One of the projects added to the list is the installation 
of a planted median along a quarter-mile of Valley Center Road, a main road in Valley Center. 
' 
You stated that a dozen businesses along the stretch of road are unified in objections to 
placing the planted median installation project on the CIP list. The concern is that the planted 
median will require business customers to make U-tums at designated areas, as opposed to the flat 
asphalt that currently allows customers to tum into businesses directly. You stated that the 
installation of a planted median will result in a direct and significant impact on those businesses. 
You and your wife currently lease part of a building for your business, Inspirations Quilt 
Shop (the "Quilt Shop"). The Quilt Shop is located in a building along the road where the planted 
median would be installed. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 82048 defines a "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a 
state or local government ag~ncy. Regulation 18700, subdivision (c), provides that a "public 
official" does not include members of a group that does not have decision-making authority. While 
the VCCPG is identified as an advisory board, its members are elected, adhere to the Brown Act, 
file Form 700 disclosures, perform county training once a year, and take ethics training courses 
every two years, which tends to indicate its members have decision-making authority and, 
therefore, qualify as public officials. 
Specific to your facts, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest 
may arise, including: 
• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• 
An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
An interest in a source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 8710~(c).) 


File No. A-18-200 
Page No. 3 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Based on the facts you provided, you have interests in the Quilt Shop as a business entity 
and as a source of income. Assuming you have a $2,000 investment in the lease, you also have a 
real property interest. You also have an interest in your personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable the 
decision will have a material financial effect on an official's interest. The standard for foreseeability 
varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a decision. An official's financial 
interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision, and is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the 
official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).)2 For a financial interest that is not explicitly 
involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) Your interests are not explicitly involved in VCCPG's decision. 
For purposes of you interests in the Quilt Shop as a business entity and source of income, 
Regulation 18702.1 (b )3 provides, in relevant part: 
"[T]he financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient 
information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial 
effect would contribute to a change in the price of the ... value of a privately-held 
business entity. Examples of decisions that may be applicable include those that: 
"(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as 
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may 
affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity 
receives." 
The planted median project involves traffic improvements to the quarter-mile portion of 
road that fronts the Quilt Shop. You noted that the installation of a raised, planted median would 
impact the amount of business the businesses receive, including the Quilt Sl}op, given the alteration 
to current road configurations. Therefore, based on the facts you provided, it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the VCCPG's decision to place the planted median project on the CIP list will have 
2 A financial interest is the subject ofa proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes 
any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
3 See also Regulation l 8702.3(a)(4). 


File No. A-18-200 
Page No. 4 
a material financial effect on your interests in the Quilt Shop.4 Accordingly, you have a conflict of 
interest in a governmental decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
By: 
Counsel, Legal Division 
SKP:gal 
4 Given that we determined you have a disqualifying conflict of interest resulting from your interests in the 
Quilt Shop as a business entity and source of income, we do not analyze your leasehold interest or your interest in your 
personal finances.

3 See also Regulation l 8702.3(a)(4).
